questions: Within the statewide network that operates California’s primary poison control services, several physicians serve as division-level medical directors while also holding academic appointments. Identify two such physician leaders from different regional divisions of this system and compare their primary academic affiliations, board certifications, and main clinical or research focus areas. How do their professional backgrounds reflect the mission of a poison control system in California?

logic_chain: Amazon.com: Poisoning and Drug Overdose, Eighth Edition eBook : Anderson, Ilene B., Benowitz, Neal L., Blanc, Paul D., Kim-Katz, Susan Y., Lewis, Justin C., Wu, Alan H. B., Olson, Kent R., Smollin, Craig: Kindle Store
rounds.question: Within the statewide network that operates California’s primary poison control services, several physicians serve as division-level medical directors while also holding academic appointments. Identify two such physician leaders from different regional divisions of this system and compare their primary academic affiliations, board certifications, and main clinical or research focus areas. How do their professional backgrounds reflect the mission of a poison control system in California?
checklist_width: Rais Vohra – Academic affiliation (University of California, San Francisco / Fresno program)
Rais Vohra – Board certification in Emergency Medicine and Medical Toxicology
Rais Vohra – Clinical/research focus on emergency medicine, medical toxicology, and public health preparedness
Richard Clark – Academic affiliation (University of California, San Diego)
Richard Clark – Board certification in Emergency Medicine and Medical Toxicology
Richard Clark – Clinical/research focus on toxicology, overdose management, and poison center operations
checklist_depth: Rais Vohra – Identified as a Medical Director within the California Poison Control System (Fresno/Madera Division) + proof of leadership role in the statewide poison control network
Richard Clark – Identified as a Medical Director within the California Poison Control System (San Diego Division) + proof of leadership role in the statewide poison control network
rounds.rationale: The question uses the logical filter of physicians who serve as division-level medical directors within California’s statewide poison control system (Deep reasoning). It does not name the individuals, requiring the search agent to identify qualified healthcare professionals through the organizational structure. It then requires aggregation of multiple publicly verifiable details—academic appointments, board certifications, and research/clinical focus—for two separate leaders (Wide scope), ensuring comparison across multiple sources rather than reliance on a single document.
source_nodes: Amazon.com: Poisoning and Drug Overdose, Eighth Edition eBook : Anderson, Ilene B., Benowitz, Neal L., Blanc, Paul D., Kim-Katz, Susan Y., Lewis, Justin C., Wu, Alan H. B., Olson, Kent R., Smollin, Craig: Kindle Store
Who We Serve | California Poison Control System
rounds.answer_a:
**Dr. Livshits** (San Francisco Division) and **Dr. Daniel Lasoff** (San Diego Division) exemplify physician leaders advancing California's poison control mission through academic-clinical integration.

Dr. Livshits serves as Assistant Medical Director of the California Poison Control System's San Francisco Division while holding the academic title of Clinical Professor in Emergency Medicine and Medical Toxicology at UCSF[3]. She directs the UCSF Medical Toxicology Fellowship and maintains clinical practice as an Attending Physician in UCSF's Department of Emergency Medicine[3]. Her board certification in emergency medicine and medical toxicology positions her to train the next generation of toxicologists while providing expert clinical consultation.

Dr. Lasoff, as Assistant Director at the California Poison Control System's San Diego site, serves as Associate Clinical Professor of Emergency Medicine at UC San Diego and directs the UC San Diego Medical Toxicology Fellowship[3]. His academic dual appointment similarly bridges clinical toxicology practice with formal training programs.

Both physicians demonstrate how poison control medical directors integrate three critical functions: direct patient consultation, physician education, and workforce development through fellowship training. Their positions reflect California's organizational model, where poison control divisions operate within academic medical centers, enabling evidence-based practice standards[1].

Their focus on medical toxicology fellowships directly supports poison control's mission by ensuring a sustainable pipeline of specialists equipped to handle complex poisoning cases. Dr. Livshits's emergency medicine background at a major academic center mirrors Dr. Lasoff's similar trajectory, demonstrating that clinical expertise in both emergency medicine and toxicology is essential for regional division leadership.

These leaders exemplify how poison control systems function as both clinical safety networks and research-training platforms, integrating public health protection with academic advancement and ensuring California maintains expert capacity to respond to toxic exposures across diverse populations[5].

## References

[1] https://www.clintox.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/Executive-Director-of-the-California-Poison-Control-System.pdf

[3] https://www.acmt.net/facmt/

[5] https://calpoison.org
rounds.answer_b:
Dr. Richard Clark and Dr. Rais Vohra exemplify the physician leadership within California’s Poison Control System (CPCS), directing the San Diego and Fresno divisions, respectively [1, 2]. Dr. Clark, a Professor of Emergency Medicine at UC San Diego, and Dr. Vohra, a Professor of Clinical Medicine at UCSF Fresno, both maintain primary academic affiliations that integrate clinical service with medical education [1, 2]. Both physicians hold dual board certifications in Emergency Medicine and Medical Toxicology, equipping them to manage acute poisoning events while providing specialized public health guidance [1, 2].

Their professional focuses diverge to address different critical needs. Dr. Clark’s research concentrates on envenomations, pharmacology of abused drugs, and occupational medicine, tackling specific biological and chemical threats [1]. In contrast, Dr. Vohra emphasizes the opioid epidemic, substance use disorders, and preventative community health strategies, targeting the social determinants of health [2].

These backgrounds directly support the CPCS mission of saving lives and reducing injury through expert advice and innovation. Clark’s expertise in acute toxicology ensures high-quality response to complex exposures, while Vohra’s public health approach aids in mitigating statewide epidemics like opioid misuse. Their academic roles allow them to conduct research that informs state health policy, ensuring the system remains robust in the face of evolving medical challenges.

## References
[1] https://health.ucsd.edu/specialties/emergency/about/Pages/richard-clark.aspx
[2] https://www.ucsf.edu/news/2020/01/416506/toxicologist-dr-rais-vohra-named-california-poison-control-medical-director-fresno
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent B correctly identifies Dr. Richard Clark (San Diego Division) and Dr. Rais Vohra (Fresno Division) as division-level medical directors within the California Poison Control System, satisfying the leadership requirement. Agent A fails this core requirement by selecting Dr. Livshits and Dr. Lasoff, who are not the target physician leaders specified in the checklist. This is a DEEP failure.

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent B covers academic affiliations (UC San Diego; UCSF Fresno), dual board certifications (Emergency Medicine and Medical Toxicology), and distinct research/clinical focus areas (envenomations, drug pharmacology, occupational medicine vs. opioid epidemic and public health). Agent A provides board certifications and academic roles, but for the wrong individuals—thus failing the WIDE checklist as well.

Finally, User Experience: While Agent A has slightly stronger formatting and structured emphasis, accuracy is foundational. Agent B delivers the correct entities, comparative structure, and clear synthesis tying their backgrounds to the poison control mission. Because Agent A failed both entity identification and required detail alignment, and Agent B satisfied all core criteria, the correct judgment is B_MUCH_BETTER.
